identity.title: Nature Parks and Other Similar Institutions

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms operating preserved natural areas, nature centers, wildlife sanctuaries, natural-wonder attractions such as caverns and waterfalls, and similar settings that repeatedly serve paying visitors, members, schools, or program customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Government park agencies, conservation-program administration, passive landholding without a recurring external-customer service, captive preserves, commercial hunting or fishing preserves classified elsewhere, accommodation-led properties classified elsewhere, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Mostly posted-price admissions, tours, passes, memberships, education and events, with ancillary food and merchandise; nonprofit operators also rely on donations and grants. Customers are predominantly individuals, families, members, schools, and organized groups.

a.bridge: O*NET and NPS describe park-naturalist and park operating tasks rather than a wage-weighted six-digit industry mix, while ILO measures generic task exposure across occupations. I classify written interpretation, trip planning, routine visitor questions, fee and permit support, scheduling, marketing, membership communications, reporting, and data analysis as partly AI-exposed, but treat trail and facility maintenance, field surveys, emergency response, resource protection, guided engagement, and most on-site safety work as lightly exposed. Government-park job descriptions may overstate regulatory and emergency work relative to private attractions, while task-level GenAI studies may overstate substitutability.
a.rationale: Nature-park labor combines a meaningful information and administration layer with a large physical, outdoor, scientific, and visitor-accountability core. AI can accelerate content, communications, reservations, routine support, records, and analysis, but cannot perform most land stewardship, maintenance, patrol, field observation, rescue, or in-person interpretation.
a.change_evidence: A current private-only 712190 OEWS occupation-and-wage extract joined to O*NET task ratings, plus payroll-hour studies from paid caverns, wildlife sanctuaries, preserves, and nature centers that separate field, maintenance, interpretation, visitor-service, and administrative work.
caveats: No current six-digit wage-weighted occupational mix was found for the eligible private population.
Federal park jobs are a functional proxy and may differ from smaller private attractions.
Generic GenAI exposure is not evidence of substitution, and the injected compensation-to-receipts ratio has the assignment's stated 2024-wage/2022-receipt vintage mismatch and IPS harmonization.

rho.bridge: NPS has already moved fee and permit workflows online and cashless in some parks, and its tourism advisers identify AI-assisted data analysis and digital visitor journeys as emerging tools. I translate this government and large-system adoption evidence to the share of exposed work that an LMM private operator can implement within five years. Government privacy and procurement constraints may make NPS slower than private firms, while poor connectivity, small technical teams, conservation obligations, and safety review can make dispersed private sites equally or more constrained.
rho.rationale: Reservations, payments, standard inquiries, content drafting, scheduling, memberships, reporting, and basic visitor analytics are implementable with existing platforms. Patchy connectivity, seasonal staffing, legacy systems, accessibility needs, data privacy, scientific accuracy, emergency response, and the value of human interpretation prevent full conversion of the exposed task pool.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor hours, conversion rates, incident logs, guest satisfaction, and error audits from 10-15 private nature attractions adopting AI-enabled CRM, contact-center, scheduling, interpretation-content, and resource-monitoring workflows.
caveats: Cashless payment and online permits demonstrate digitization rather than AI implementation.
The NPS technology discussion is a draft advisory document for a public system, not measured LMM adoption.
Implementation must preserve human escalation for weather, wildlife, accessibility, emergencies, and route-safety questions.

e.bridge: The 1997 Economic Census counted 166 establishments subject to federal income tax and 276 tax-exempt establishments in 712190, implying a 37.6% taxable share of that historical employer-establishment population. I use taxable status as an imperfect proxy for transferable private control and then bound for changes in classification, multi-establishment firms, nonprofits that may transfer operations but not equity, and taxable entities lacking the recurring-service or EBITDA-band characteristics. The old vintage creates large two-sided error.
e.rationale: Paid caverns, natural-wonder attractions, and privately operated preserves can be transferable businesses with repeat admissions and tours. Many nature centers, sanctuaries, and conservation areas are nonprofit, public, restricted by land covenants, or governed for mission rather than owned as saleable equity, limiting eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A current establishment-level linkage of the Census Business Register, IRS exempt-organization records, PAD-US ownership, state attraction licenses, land records, and beneficial ownership, filtered to the injected $1-10M normalized EBITDA band and manually checked for recurring customer revenue.
caveats: The tax-status split is from 1997 and is establishment-based, while the primitive is firm-based and current.
Taxable status is not sufficient proof of transferable control, and some nonprofit operating assets or contracts may transfer without equity ownership.
The injected LMM firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE and is not re-estimated here.

s5.bridge: The Exit Planning Institute surveyed 1,162 US owners in more than 25 industries rather than nature-park firms; 49% wanted to exit within five years, and its owner-readiness material states only 20-30% of businesses taken to market sell. I combine intent and market attrition as an anchor, then temper it for immovable natural assets, conservation restrictions, permits, environmental liabilities, and a narrow strategic-buyer pool. Self-selected respondents may overstate near-term intent, and sale success among marketed businesses is not a direct five-year hazard.
s5.rationale: Eligible paid attractions are often closely held and can face succession, but natural-resource dependence, specialized sites, seasonality, land-use constraints, and limited buyers reduce the probability that an intended exit becomes a qualifying control transfer in five years.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed 712190 deals from DealStats, BizBuySell, GF Data, PitchBook, county deed records, state permit transfers, and Form 990 asset dispositions, coded to separate business control sales from land donations, government acquisitions, nonprofit reorganizations, and internal succession.
caveats: The owner survey is cross-industry and self-reported, with no nature-park subsample.
A real-estate conveyance may not preserve a transferable operating service, while a concession or lease may change hands without the underlying land.
Internal restructurings and deaths without transferable operations are excluded.

q.bridge: For taxable 712190 establishments in the 1997 Economic Census, admissions were 66.7% of receipts, food and beverage 6.9%, merchandise 21.7%, and other receipts 4.6%; NAPCS also defines single and multiple admissions, season passes, and access-oriented memberships for this industry. I map that fixed, posted-price mix to five-year benefit retention for today's private LMM operators. The very old revenue mix may overstate gate dependence if memberships, digital channels, events, or concessions have grown, and mission or competitive pricing can push retention below the mechanical billing implication.
q.rationale: Admission, tour, pass, event, and merchandise prices do not automatically fall when back-office or visitor-support costs decline, supporting retention. Leakage comes through competitive pricing, discounting, improved service, wage sharing, maintenance reinvestment, and attendance elasticity; grant- or donor-supported programs offer less direct monetization.
q.change_evidence: Current private-operator revenue mix and five-year price histories for admissions, tours, passes, school programs, events, concessions, and memberships, paired with visitor elasticity and competitor-response data after documented workflow savings.
caveats: The only detailed six-digit revenue mix located is from 1997.
Historic taxable firms may differ from today's eligible EBITDA-band operators in size, attraction type, and ancillary revenue.
Savings may be required for conservation, safety, trail, or facility reinvestment rather than retained as distributable margin.

d5.bridge: BEA reports that real US outdoor-recreation gross output grew 2.0% in 2024 and real GDP grew 2.7%, while NPS recorded 323 million recreation visits in 2025 and maintains a long-running visitation series. I use those broader activity and public-park measures as directional anchors for five-year constant-price demand at private nature attractions, not as a direct forecast. The broad outdoor economy includes equipment, travel, hospitality, and other activities and likely overstates direct attraction growth; NPS visitation may understate or overstate private demand depending on local substitution and tourism trends.
d5.rationale: Demand for outdoor recreation, natural wonders, education, and local nature access appears durable, with modest real growth as a reasonable base. The range crosses 1.0 because the service is discretionary and exposed to recession, weather, wildfire, flooding, disease, travel costs, and site-specific closures.
d5.change_evidence: A 2019-2026 matched panel of admissions, tours, member visits, and school-program units for private 712190 sites, deflated by service price and segmented by local versus tourist demand, plus site-level booking forecasts and climate-closure histories.
caveats: Outdoor-recreation output is far broader than 712190 and includes substantial supporting travel and hospitality activity.
NPS visitation measures public parks and counts visits, not private paid-service quantity or unique customers.
A single flood, fire, access failure, or ecological restriction can dominate demand for an individual site.

o.rationale: The current service basket depends on an accountable operator preserving the natural setting, managing access, maintaining trails and facilities, monitoring resources, handling safety and incidents, and providing credible interpretation. Self-service reservations, digital wayfinding, virtual content, and unattended access can remove some staffed interactions, but software cannot assume land stewardship, environmental compliance, liability, emergency readiness, or physical maintenance.
o.change_evidence: Visitor substitution studies for virtual versus physical nature experiences, self-service adoption and unattended-entry data, insurer and regulator requirements by attraction type, and longitudinal closures or conversions that identify service quantities eliminated rather than merely delivered through a new channel.
caveats: This is bounded judgment, not a measured operator-required share.
Operator intensity varies sharply between a guided cavern or wildlife sanctuary and a lightly serviced preserve.
Digital self-guidance may reduce front-line labor without removing the accountable operating entity.

narrative.executive_view: Nature parks pair a high-compensation operating model with an automatable information layer, but the core product is physical stewardship and access to a site. AI can improve reservations, visitor support, content, schedules, memberships, reporting, and resource data analysis. Transferability is limited by nonprofit ownership, land and conservation restrictions, and highly site-specific assets.
narrative.ai_impact: The most implementable uses are trip-planning and FAQ support, fee and permit workflows, marketing and interpretive content drafts, scheduling, member communications, records, and analytics. Maintenance, patrol, field surveys, habitat work, emergency response, guided engagement, and safety decisions remain human and on-site.
narrative.value_capture: Private nature attractions historically earned most receipts from posted-price admissions and ancillary merchandise, a structure that does not mechanically pass efficiency savings to visitors. Retention is reduced by competitive discounts, attendance elasticity, wages, conservation duties, and the need to reinvest in trails, facilities, and safety.
narrative.firm_availability: Paid caverns and natural-wonder attractions can be conventional closely held businesses, but many nature centers, preserves, and sanctuaries are tax-exempt, public, or constrained by mission and land covenants. Even a willing owner may face a thin buyer set and complex environmental, permit, title, or concession diligence.
narrative.demand_durability: Current public visitation and broad real outdoor-recreation output support stable-to-modestly-growing demand for physical nature experiences. Demand remains discretionary and unusually vulnerable to local weather, wildfire, flood, ecosystem protection, road access, tourism cycles, and catastrophic closure.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main gaps are a current private six-digit occupation mix, current taxable-versus-exempt ownership by EBITDA band, modern revenue mix, and closed transaction history. The most precise industry revenue and tax-status evidence is from 1997, while current workflow and demand sources mostly describe public parks or the broader outdoor economy.
narrative.principal_driver: Posted-price admissions and tours can preserve much of the value from automating visitor-information and administrative workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Site stewardship, safety, and maintenance remain physical, while nonprofit ownership and land restrictions sharply narrow transferable supply.

sources.title: NAICS 712190 Nature Parks and Other Similar Institutions
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments preserving and exhibiting natural areas or settings, including sanctuaries, conservation areas, natural-wonder attractions, nature centers and preserves, and national parks.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Park Naturalists
sources.supports: Lists visitor service, facility operation, program scheduling and delivery, event planning, staff supervision, emergency duty, curriculum, displays, research, writing, records, photography, maintenance, animal care, and field-survey tasks.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: A Refined Global Index of Occupational Exposure
sources.supports: Uses task evidence and expert validation to estimate GenAI exposure; finds clerical occupations most exposed while full automation remains limited, supporting task-level rather than whole-job interpretation.

sources.title: National Park Service Careers: Interpretation, Education, and Visitor Services
sources.supports: Describes park work spanning visitor desks, calls, email, websites, fees, programs, trails, curricula, visual information, volunteers, scheduling, and in-person public engagement.

sources.title: Black Canyon Moves to Cashless Fee Collection
sources.supports: Reports that electronic payment reduces transaction time and cash-processing work and that online entrance, camping, reservation, and permit payments are available across hundreds of federal recreation sites.

sources.title: Draft Recommendations: Strategies for Advancing Stewardship, Tourism, Conservation, and Partnerships
sources.supports: Describes digital visitor journeys, mobile and contactless service, AI-enabled data analysis, and constraints involving privacy, regulation, useful data, user preferences, and park-specific tailoring.

sources.title: Forest and Conservation Workers, Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Documents physical conservation work including planting, trail and campground clearing, measuring trees, vegetation treatment, fire prevention and suppression, equipment maintenance, and cleaning public facilities.

sources.title: 1997 Economic Census: Arts, Entertainment, and Recreation Subject Series Summary
sources.supports: For 712190, reports 166 taxable and 276 tax-exempt employer establishments; taxable receipts were 66.7% admissions, 6.9% food and beverage, 21.7% merchandise, and 4.6% other, while tax-exempt revenue included 33.8% memberships and 5.2% admissions.

sources.title: 2023 National State of Owner Readiness Report
sources.supports: Surveys 1,162 US business owners across more than 25 industries and reports that 49% want to exit within five years, with substantial family ownership and internal-exit preferences.

sources.title: NAPCS Product List for NAICS 712
sources.supports: Defines 712190 admission products to include single and multiple tickets, season passes, guided-tour or bundled benefits, and access-oriented memberships, and separately defines cultural-institution memberships.

sources.title: Outdoor Recreation Economic Statistics, U.S. and States, 2024
sources.supports: Reports 2024 real outdoor-recreation GDP growth of 2.7%, real gross-output growth of 2.0%, employment growth of 1.1%, and the broader composition of conventional, other, and supporting outdoor activities.

sources.title: National Park Service Visitor Use Data
sources.supports: Reports 323 million National Park Service recreation visits in calendar 2025 and describes the annual and monthly historical visitor-use data series.
